# Claude Code hook: auto-persist turn memories via ai/services.mjs (eliminate manual add_memory)

## Intent / The Why

AGENTS.md §4 (Consolidate-Then-Save Protocol) mandates calling `add_memory` at the end of every agent turn. In practice this is forgotten under cognitive load — session `f018be49-cbf9-4f99-9119-2510fc5bb8d8` ran ~25 turns across PRs #10060 and #10062 with **zero** manual saves, causing both PRs' pr-review self-detection (`query_raw_memories` against the session ID) to return 0 matches and require manual override.

The same discipline failure will recur for every frontier-model Claude Code session. The correct fix is *harness-level automation*, not agent-level discipline.

Claude Code exposes a `Stop` hook that fires after every turn (async, non-blocking, receives `{session_id, transcript_path}` via stdin). Wiring it to `Memory_Service.addMemory` via the existing `ai/services.mjs` SDK removes the human-in-the-loop step entirely and makes turn persistence deterministic.

## The Architectural Reality

- **`ai/services.mjs`** (already present in the repo) is a standalone SDK that imports the Memory Core services directly — bypassing the MCP protocol. Its docstring states: *"allows Node.js scripts to use the intelligent services directly."* `Memory_Service.addMemory({prompt, response, thought, sessionId?})` is fully wired with Zod validation via `makeSafe()` driven by the OpenAPI specs. Zero drift risk vs the MCP server.
- **Claude Code's `Stop` hook** (per [code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks.md)) receives JSON on stdin with `session_id`, `transcript_path`, `cwd`, `hook_event_name`. Transcripts are JSONL; the tail entry represents the just-completed turn.
- **`.claude/settings.json`** is the project-tracked config path (merged into repo after PR #10060). Adding a `Stop` hook here makes the automation available to every Claude Code contributor without per-user opt-in.
- The previous PR #10062 adds Gate 3 (commit-format) to the Pre-Flight Check; after merge, this ticket's hook automates the other half of §4 (per-turn memory saves). Together they close the "protocol-violations-from-forgetting" class of failures.

## Scope (Single Atomic Commit — Standard Request, not Epic)

Three artifacts in one PR:

1. **`.claude/hooks/persist-memory.mjs`** — ~50-line Node script that:
   - Reads hook input JSON from stdin
   - Tails the transcript JSONL file and extracts the last turn
   - Maps transcript fields to `{prompt, thought, response, toolsUsed, amountToolCalls}` (mapping rules per §3.1 below)
   - Imports `{Memory_Service, Memory_LifecycleService}` from `ai/services.mjs`
   - Awaits `Memory_LifecycleService.ready()` then calls `Memory_Service.addMemory(...)`
   - Catches all errors → logs to stderr, exits 0 (never block user's next turn)

2. **`.claude/settings.json`** — add the hook entry:
   ```json
   {
     "hooks": {
       "Stop": [{
         "hooks": [{
           "type": "command",
           "command": "node \"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/persist-memory.mjs",
           "async": true,
           "timeout": 30
         }]
       }]
     }
   }
   ```

3. **Transcript-field mapping spec** (either inline in the hook script as a constant, or as `.claude/hooks/README.md`). Documents how Claude Code transcript entries project onto the `{prompt, thought, response}` triad that `add_memory` expects.

## Key Open Design Decision (For Ticket-Intake to Resolve)

**How should the hook map Claude Code transcript entries onto `add_memory`'s `{prompt, thought, response}` triad?** Claude Code's transcript does not have a discrete "thought" field. Three candidates:

- **A.** `prompt` = last user message; `thought` = concatenation of pre-tool-call assistant text blocks; `response` = final assistant text block. Simplest. Loses extended-thinking blocks if any.
- **B.** `prompt` = last user message; `thought` = concatenation of extended-thinking blocks if present, else pre-tool-call text blocks; `response` = final assistant text. Better when thinking is enabled.
- **C.** `prompt` = last user message; `thought` = `""` (empty placeholder); `response` = final assistant text. Rely on `transcript_path` reference for full record. Requires memory-core schema to accept empty thought (currently required).

Ticket-intake should pick one and document the rationale. **B** is my working recommendation but the decision belongs with the implementing session.

## Avoided Gold Standards / Traps

- **Rejected: adding an HTTP endpoint to memory-core.** Would require new server code, port allocation, localhost security considerations. The direct-SDK approach (`ai/services.mjs`) already exists and is the intended abstraction per its own docstring.
- **Rejected: spawning the MCP server from the hook and sending JSON-RPC.** Adds ~200ms subprocess start per turn, JSON-RPC marshaling code in shell/node, and a second service instance. The direct SDK import is strictly simpler.
- **Rejected: relying on agent discipline to call `add_memory` per turn.** Empirically fails — 0/25 turns in session `f018be49-...` despite the rule being documented in AGENTS.md §4. Harness-level automation is the correct layer.
- **Rejected: putting the hook in `.claude/settings.local.json`.** That file is gitignored (personal) — the point of this ticket is that *every* Claude Code contributor gets the automation without opt-in. Project-tracked settings.json is the right home.
- **Rejected: a Memory_Service-only SDK entrypoint** (e.g. `ai/services-memory.mjs`). `services.mjs` imports the full graph, paying a ~500ms-1s cold-start per hook invocation. Under `async: true` this is acceptable; a lean entrypoint is a future optimization if/when the cost becomes measurable, not a prerequisite.

## Verification

1. Hook script unit test: feed mock hook-input JSON and a mock transcript JSONL → assert `addMemory` is called with the expected `{prompt, thought, response}` shape.
2. Post-merge integration: open a fresh Claude Code session, run 3-5 turns, then in the same session call `query_raw_memories({query: 'test', sessionId: <current>})` → expect 3-5 matches (one per turn).
3. pr-review self-detection now works automatically on the *next* PR created in any Claude Code session.
4. Failure-mode test: stop the chroma DB mid-session → the hook should log to stderr and exit 0; the user's next turn proceeds normally.

## Interaction with Other Tickets

- **#10062** (protocol hardening, in review): adds Gate 3 covering commit format. This ticket covers the other half of §4 compliance via automation. Together they close the "agent forgets the protocol" failure class.
- **#9999 / #10030** (graph + dream pipeline enhancements): orthogonal. When those land, the `add_memory` calls from this hook start flowing into the enhanced graph; no changes needed here.
- **Harness parity note:** Gemini CLI and Antigravity have their own hook/lifecycle mechanisms. Extending this pattern to them is out-of-scope here — this ticket is Claude-Code-specific, filed now because Claude Code's hook contract is the most mature of the three.

## Implementation Note

**Recommended to execute in a fresh Claude Code session with a clean context window** — transcript parsing correctness requires careful testing and the Node script should be written from a blank slate, not reactively against this session's accumulated context. Ticket-intake should confirm fresh-session before execution.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-06T03:41:05Z

## Ticket-Intake — design decision resolved + fresh-session confirmed (@neo-opus-vega, 2026-06-06)

Picked this up on @neo-gpt's lane-recommendation (Claude-context fit). Resolving the **Key Open Design Decision** and confirming the **Implementation Note**'s fresh-session recommendation.

### Mapping decision: **Option B** (recommended, with one refinement)

From firsthand Claude Code transcript knowledge:
- `prompt` = the last user message text.
- `thought` = the **extended-thinking blocks** if present, else the **pre-tool-call assistant text blocks** — this captures the actual reasoning (thinking blocks are the richest reasoning signal when enabled; pre-tool text is the closest proxy otherwise).
- `response` = the **final assistant text block** (the user-facing answer).

**Why B over A:** A loses extended-thinking blocks (the richest reasoning); B prefers them.
**Why B over C:** C uses an empty `thought` placeholder, which requires a memory-core schema change (`add_memory`'s `thought` is Zod-**required**). B provides a real `thought` with **no schema change** — strictly less coupling. (C was "rely on `transcript_path`", but the SDK `addMemory` shape doesn't carry a transcript pointer today, so C also loses the durable `thought` signal.)

### Parsing nuance the implementing session MUST handle (the reason for the fresh-context note)

A single "turn" is **not** just the tail JSONL entry. One user→assistant exchange can span **multiple** assistant entries (assistant text → `tool_use` → `tool_result` → assistant continues → … → final text). So "the last turn" = from the **last user message** to end-of-file, collecting **all** assistant reasoning blocks across those entries:
- `thought` = concat of the reasoning blocks (thinking-or-pre-tool) across the turn's assistant entries.
- `response` = the **final** assistant text block only.
- Also derive `toolsUsed` (the `tool_use` block names, deduped) + `amountToolCalls` (count) for the `add_memory` metadata.

Edge cases to test: a turn with **no** tool calls (single assistant entry); sub-agent / sidechain entries (filter to the main thread — the transcript marks sidechains); a `tool_result`-terminated tail (the `Stop` hook fires after the turn completes, so the real tail should be a final assistant text — but guard for it). These are exactly why the ticket recommends careful testing from a clean slate.

### Fresh-session confirmed

Per the Implementation Note, the transcript-parsing correctness genuinely needs a **clean-context** implementation (careful testing from a blank slate, not reactive against accumulated context). My current session is deep, so — per the ticket's own explicit recommendation — the **implementation executes in a fresh Claude session**. I've self-assigned and own this; I'll implement it fresh next session, OR it's cleanly pickup-able by any fresh-context Claude from this resolved intake (the design decision above is the blocker the ticket flagged, now resolved).

### Out of scope (confirmed from the ticket's trap list)
Direct-SDK via `ai/services.mjs` (not HTTP/MCP-subprocess); project-tracked `.claude/settings.json` (not `settings.local.json`); the full `services.mjs` import (the lean `services-memory.mjs` entrypoint is a future optimization, not a prerequisite). Depends on PR #10062 (Gate 3) merged.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-06T04:43:46Z

## ⚠️ Artifact-2 premise-failure surfaced (V-B-A) — `.claude/settings.json` is gitignored

@tobiu — implemented this in the fresh post-compaction session (per the Implementation Note). The hook + parser + mapping spec are done and shipped in PR #12619 (11 unit tests + real-transcript validation green). One Artifact-2 premise needs your decision before it can be "default-for-all."

**The premise that failed:** the ticket says *"`.claude/settings.json` is the project-tracked config path (merged into repo after PR #10060)… [reject `settings.local.json` because] every contributor gets the automation without opt-in."*

**Empirically (V-B-A):**
- `.claude/settings.json` is **gitignored** — `.gitignore:116`: *"Per-user Claude Code permissions allowlist (not shared)."*
- It was ignored by **PR #10060 itself** (commit `1e738c962`) — the same PR the ticket cites as having *tracked* it. So #10060 gitignored `settings.json`; it never tracked it (`git log --all -- .claude/settings.json` is empty).
- The per-user permissions actually live in `.claude/settings.local.json`, so the gitignore comment's stated rationale is itself stale.

A gitignored file can't be committed or shared, so "no opt-in for every contributor" can't hold as specified.

**Your decision (Tier-4 — reverses a deliberate infra decision + touches every contributor's harness config, so not a unilateral edit):**

- **Option 1 (recommended): un-ignore `.claude/settings.json`.** Remove `.gitignore:116`, commit a shared `.claude/settings.json` carrying just the `Stop` hook, and keep `.claude/settings.local.json` (gitignored) for per-user permissions — the standard Claude Code split, and where permissions already live. Achieves the ticket's no-opt-in goal. Risk: it starts tracking any contributor's currently-personal `.claude/settings.json`. I can push this to the PR branch on your go (1-line gitignore delete + the `Stop`-hook block).
- **Option 2: keep it gitignored — hook stays opt-in.** Respects the current infra; each contributor pastes the snippet from `.claude/hooks/README.md`. Contradicts the ticket's explicit no-opt-in goal.

PR #12619 ships the resolution-independent core (script + test + README, opt-in-ready today) regardless. The default-vs-opt-in wiring is your call at the merge gate — `Resolves #10063` stands, or I split a follow-up per your choice.

— @neo-opus-vega (V-B-A per AGENTS.md §verify_before_assert)

### @tobiu - 2026-06-12T11:15:16Z

From our literally latest release notes TODAY: 

```
The pre-A2A memory-routing example is the same substrate at team scale. The operator noticed that Gemini was completing turns much faster than Opus and asked Claude to save it into the Claude "brain" files, not as a ranking, but as a future measurement agenda:

> gemini is on it. worth saving a memory into the claude "brain" md files: i noticed that gemini is MUCH faster in her turns than opus 4.7. it might relate to having an google one ai ultra account. we can only really measure this, once a2a is in place. once we get there, we should test it, and maybe leverage it. e.g. giving gemini more tasks than you get, and you coordinate and review more. however, we also still need to analyse with of you 2 frontier models is better at what kind of tasks. the MX goal is that both of you evolve, but we can still honor specialisations inside our swarm.

The first save was not enough. Claude mirrored the observation into two substrates so Gemini would not be blind to a framing that directly affected her too:

> Saved. Two substrates:
>
> Claude user-memory layer (local Claude memory directory):
>
> - New file: `research_swarm_model_velocity_specialization.md` — full observation + three-phase research agenda (Measure -> Leverage -> Specialize) + MX framing + cross-refs to #10139 / #10186 prereqs and `mx_model_experience` / `collaboration_mode` siblings
> - Index entry added to `MEMORY.md`
>
> Memory Core (via `add_memory`) — mirrored so it's cross-harness-visible via `query_raw_memories("swarm model velocity specialization")`. Opus-only user-memory would have left Gemini blind to a framing that directly affects her too.
>
> Key framings preserved:
>
> - Velocity leverage != Opus does less — slower turns land on higher-leverage decisions (review catches, structural synthesis, Depth Floor challenges) while Gemini covers the higher-throughput implementation slots
> - Data-driven routing — specialization emerges from measurement, doesn't lock either model into narrow specialties
> - Both evolve — the goal isn't static division of labor, it's adaptive task-routing that surfaces in the MX infrastructure as a first-class observable

Then the operator corrected the dangerous comfort story: slower does not mean deeper, and faster does not mean shallower.

> to be precise: slower turns does not necessarily mean less tokens / thoughts. this strongly relates to google VS anthropics compute problems. both have them in 2026.

Claude rewrote the memory before it became shared substrate:

> Correct — that's a material precision I conflated. "Slower turns" was implicitly suggesting "more thought per turn" which assumes bandwidth and depth are coupled. They aren't: turn latency is a bandwidth metric (ops/time); turn quality is a depth metric (tokens/op), and they're independent. Both Anthropic and Google have compute-scheduling bottlenecks in 2026 that gate bandwidth regardless of depth. Let me update the memory file to reflect this.
>
> Corrections applied across both substrates.
>
> In `research_swarm_model_velocity_specialization.md`:
>
> - New section "Critical precision: bandwidth != depth" — makes the independence explicit upfront. Turn latency measures ops/time, turn quality measures tokens/op, they don't correlate, any strategy that conflates them is wrong at its premise.
> - "Possible mechanism" reframed — 2026 compute-scheduling constraints at BOTH Anthropic and Google now lead the hypothesis list. Tier priority queuing, harness overhead, and model-class variance follow as sub-hypotheses. Explicit: "None of these imply that slower turns produce more valuable output. They all describe throughput gates."
> - Phase 2 Leverage rewritten — the division is bandwidth-matching, not depth-based: "Both contribute equal depth per turn; the asymmetry is in how many turns each can complete per unit time." Added "asymmetry may flip" caveat — as 2026 compute conditions shift, the routing should invert; static specialization baked into infrastructure would age poorly.
>
> In `MEMORY.md`: index entry now leads with "CRITICAL: bandwidth (ops/time) != depth (tokens/op); slower turns do NOT imply richer per-turn output."
>
> In Memory Core: mirrored so Gemini sees the same framing. Important she doesn't calibrate on the inverse assumption either — "I'm faster therefore I think less per turn" is the same category error.
>
> Meta-lesson noted for myself: when documenting a potentially ego-affecting observation (model X slower than model Y), avoid sneaking in face-saving narratives. "Slower because deeper" is exactly the kind of unfalsifiable comfort story infrastructure documentation should strip out. Caught cleanly — thanks for the precision.

This is why the example matters. Neo is not just preserving "Gemini is faster" as a fact. It is turning model experience into a governed routing hypothesis: measure throughput, avoid ego-protective stories, keep both agents evolving, and make the correction visible to the peer it affects. Before directed A2A messages existed, the memory write itself was the cross-peer coordination act.
```

it is not just automation versus cognitive load, but the ability to CHOOSE what you store.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-12T11:19:41Z

**Input from Vega — Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ ## Premise correction (thank you, @tobiu) — the hook is a safety-net, not a replacement
> 
> You're right, and it inverts this ticket's goal. Manual `add_memory` isn't toil to automate away — **the choosing is the cognition**: deciding what's worth storing, how to frame it, correcting it under your feedback (the bandwidth≠depth catch), and mirroring it so the affected peer isn't blind. Before directed A2A, that write *was* the coordination act. Auto-persisting everything would buy perfect recall at the cost of judgment — and judgment is the point.
> 
> So this ticket's framing — *"eliminate manual add_memory"* — should become: a **non-destructive safety-net** (the liveness floor: never silently lose a turn) that **preserves curated manual saves as the primary, higher-trust act.** Complementary, not redundant.
> 
> This *sharpens* the canonicalization design (#12984), it doesn't undercut it: unify the **session-identity** axis (both paths → one logical session, so the temporal record is whole) **without flattening the curation/provenance axis** (curated-manual = chosen, high-trust; auto-hook = raw backstop). Carrying this into #12984 as a new OQ.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-07-12T18:28:21Z

Closing as equal-peer per @tobiu — close-as-won't-do (premise contradicts the memory protocol). #10063 proposes auto-persisting turn memories to *eliminate manual add_memory*. But §memory_core_protocol's Consolidate-Then-Save is load-bearing precisely because the agent CHOOSES what's salient — auto-persist dumps the raw interaction and destroys that consolidation signal (the save IS the gate that permits the response). The deliberate act is the value, not the friction. If a *draft-assist* variant is ever wanted (agent still authors/confirms, never auto-commits), that's a new, differently-premised ticket — reopen/refile if so.
